articles.title: Medical students are using popular research tool to pump out misleading studies
articles.summary: Medical students are relying on a widely used research tool to generate false or misleading study results. The tool, which is often used in medical research, can produce fake data that is presented as real, leading to incorrect conclusions and potentially harming patients. This issue highlights the importance of verifying information through reputable sources before accepting it as true. As a result, developers are being called upon to create more robust tools for vetting research findings.

articles.title: Zombie unicorns are haunting Silicon Valley

The malware, which has been found in several major operating systems, uses advanced techniques to evade detection and spread quickly through networks. As a result, it is likely to cause significant disruptions to critical infrastructure in Silicon Valley. The implications for developers who rely on virtualization technology are substantial.

articles.title: Are We GlobalShortcuts Yet?
articles.summary: The integration progress of XDG GlobalShotcuts, an implementation of global keyboard shortcuts on Wayland applications, is being tracked by developers. This technology enables apps to respond to specific key combinations while unfocused without direct access to all user input events, a feature currently used by some applications that circumvent sandboxing and security defaults. By highlighting when developers move toward a secure "least privilege" model, this initiative aims to support OS defaults and promote a more secure approach to application functionality.

articles.title: Ignore DNSSEC if you like MITM attacks
articles.summary: One day around 2010, a team experimented with ARP poisoning and intercepting traffic for other hosts on their network at work, revealing that all data was being sent through the network. They also discovered that hubs and open Wi-Fi networks were still common back then, allowing them to listen to traffic without needing ARP poisoning. HTTPS was introduced around this time, but it faced resistance due to its complexity and potential for errors. The same arguments against HTTPS are often raised about DNSSEC, which can be used to prevent spoofed DNS responses by pointing email clients to secure servers.

articles.title: PR spam today looks like email spam in the early 2000s
articles.summary: PR spam today looks like email spam in the early 2000s when it comes to open source contribution, with large numbers of low-effort slop submissions flooding repositories and causing a spike in merge rates for many projects. This phenomenon is reminiscent of the ILOVEYOU worm that infected millions of computers in 24 hours due to the lack of effective filtering measures at the time. As a result, contributors are being filtered by their reputation, with certain groups being more likely to be blocked or flagged than others.

articles.title: Porting WINE to a new Hobby OS
articles.summary: Porting WINE to a new hobby OS is necessary due to the closed source nature of many games, which requires compiling for Windows, making it difficult to run on other operating systems. The Wine project has been working on this goal, but its existing port was incomplete, and additional steps were needed to make it work. To fix these issues, Wine was modified to use WoW64 mode, a feature that allows 32-bit Windows binaries to be run in a 64-bit process, translating between system calls and data structures as needed. This modification required implementing LDT support in the kernel, which is necessary for running 32-bit code in long mode using 32-bit segment descriptors.
